APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper lies squarely within NLP/ML: it tackles semantic parsing and interactive language learning to ‘naturalize’ a core programming language (sections on definitions, chart extension via alignment, and log-linear models). Methods and evaluation match venue expectations—implemented in SEMPRE, uses alignment heuristics, AdaGrad updates, and demonstrates a community user study where users built 240 complex voxel structures and shifted to the naturalized language for 74.7% of the last 10K utterances. The work is positioned against relevant prior NLP/semantic-parsing literature (Zettlemoyer & Collins, Artzi & Zettlemoyer, Pasupat & Liang, Wang et al. 2016), so the audience, problem formulation, methods, and evaluation are appropriate for a top NLP/ML conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper reports a real-world deployment on Voxelurn (240 complex structures built; naturalized language used in 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and describes concrete algorithmic components (interactive definitions, chart extension via alignment with two heuristics), which supports the main claims. However, the provided text shows no clear ablation/sensitivity studies quantifying the contribution of each component (e.g., alignment extension vs. baseline), only limited discussion of failure modes (a brief note about low-precision rules and conservative induction), and sparse reproduction detail (only that it uses SEMPRE and high-level algorithm sketches without datasets, hyperparameters, or full evaluation protocols). Related work is reasonably cited, but experimental depth and reproducibility details are insufficient for a higher score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents plausible algorithms (Algorithm 2) and concrete heuristics (exclusion, projectivity) for transplanting derivations via alignment, and reports user-driven results (240 structures, 74.7% naturalized usage) supporting the main claim that definitions scale learning. However, no formal derivations or correctness guarantees are provided for the alignment/transplantation procedure, and the heuristic decisions (when to extend, thresholds of 1–2 unmatched tokens) are not justified theoretically. Experimental methodology details are sparse in the provided text: there are no described baselines, statistical tests, or ablations quantifying precision/recall tradeoffs or the impact of conservative induction, leaving some claims (e.g., improved scope over Wang et al. (2016)) under-supported. Overall the technical approach is reasonable but some important evidence and rigor are missing.

CLARITY.justification: The excerpt generally presents a logical flow (interactive learning → definitions → alignment → discussion) and includes useful concrete examples (Figure 3 definitions) and Algorithm 2, which aid comprehension. However, the text suffers from serious presentation problems: many sentences and paragraphs are duplicated verbatim (e.g., repeated “Another route is to convert natural language…” and repeats of Wang et al. (2016)), stray LaTeX/typos (“the the”), and truncated fragments (the log-linear model paragraph is cut off), which disrupt readability. Notation is partially explained (derivation d, chart(x)) but some definitions and equations are incomplete or inconsistently presented. Overall the ideas are accessible but the editing/formatting issues make parts hard to follow.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem — making powerful programming-language actions accessible via natural language — and proposes a practical solution (interactive head/body definitions) to scale to complex action spaces that prior selection-based supervision cannot handle (e.g., the 1036 distinct denotations example). The demonstrated success with a community building 240 complex voxel structures and the naturalized language reaching 74.7% usage in the last 10K utterances suggests the approach materially changes how such interfaces can be deployed and learned. The alignment-based rule extension and the claim that the methodology applies beyond Voxelurn (questions over semi-structured data, regexes, robot instructions) indicate clear new research directions and reasonably broad applicability.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a notable extension to prior interactive learning: allowing users to provide definitions ("Each definition consists of a head utterance and a body") to naturalize a core programming language and compose higher-level concepts, which clearly goes beyond candidate-selection supervision (Wang et al., 2016). The alignment-based chart extension ("Algorithm 2: Extending the chart by alignment" with heuristics like exclusion and projectivity) is a concrete, non-trivial mechanism for generalization. While the work builds on existing semantic-parsing and rule-induction ideas (GENLEX, higher-order unification, SEMPRE) and is therefore not wholly revolutionary, the combination of interactive definitional teaching plus conservative alignment transplantation is a distinct and practically useful innovation that would inform experts.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper cites and discusses many relevant prior works (e.g., Wang et al. 2016 for interactive denotational supervision and multiple semantic parsing works such as Zettlemoyer & Collins 2005; Artzi & Zettlemoyer 2013; Kushman & Barzilay 2013; Pasupat & Liang 2015), and it conceptually contrasts its use of definitions with Wang et al. However, the provided text contains no clear empirical, metric-level comparisons or controlled experiments against these baselines, nor evidence that comparisons were run under the same data/conditions. Differences are discussed qualitatively (feasibility of candidate selection, definition-based generalization), but the lack of fair quantitative comparisons or acknowledgements when prior methods might perform similarly limits the thoroughness of the evaluation.
